title: Momentum: Rolling Downhill Faster
concept: Momentum accumulates a 'velocity' from past gradient steps, smoothing out zig-zagging and helping push through small bumps and plateaus, like a ball rolling downhill.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does plain SGD do when it hits a narrow ravine in the loss landscape (steep walls, gentle slope along the bottom)?

options.text: It zig-zags, bouncing back and forth across the ravine instead of moving smoothly toward the bottom
It instantly jumps straight to the bottom
It stops completely and never moves again

exercises.explain: Right! All that bouncing wastes steps. So how do we stop the wasted zig-zag and keep training moving forward?

cards.title: A puck vs. a snowflake
cards.body: A single snowflake stops the instant a breeze shifts. A hockey puck, once it's moving, keeps gliding in roughly the same direction even if the ice tilts a little. Which one do you want steering your deep network's training?

cards.title: Velocity: a memory of where you've been heading
cards.body: Momentum keeps a running velocity — a blend of the current slope direction and the direction of recent past steps — so every new step gets nudged toward the direction training has consistently been heading.

cards.title: Gliding across the rink
cards.body: Instead of reacting only to the ice right under your skates (like plain SGD reacting only to the current slope), momentum is like a puck that keeps gliding along its established direction — a small wobble in the ice barely changes its overall path down the rink.

cards.title: Canceling the zig-zag, keeping the forward push
cards.body: In a narrow ravine, the side-to-side parts of each step point opposite directions on alternating steps and tend to cancel out over time, while the along-the-ravine part keeps adding up — so the puck-like velocity ends up pointed straight down the ravine, far faster than plain SGD's bounce.

cards.title: Momentum doesn't guarantee a straight shot to the best answer
cards.body: You might think building up speed always helps. But too much velocity can carry the optimizer flying PAST a good minimum, overshooting it like a puck hit too hard — deep learning teams tune how much past velocity to keep, usually a large chunk, but never all of it.

cards.title: Plain SGD vs. momentum on a plateau
cards.body: How the two handle a long flat stretch:

left.text:
Tiny gradient → tiny step
Crawls to a near stall

right.text:
Carries built-up velocity
Glides straight through the flat stretch

exercises.prompt: What does momentum add to a plain gradient step to smooth out zig-zagging?

options.text: A 'velocity' built from the direction of recent past steps, blended with the current slope
A completely random extra step direction
A pause that waits for the next epoch to start

exercises.explain: Right! That accumulated velocity keeps pulling the optimizer in the direction it's consistently been heading.

exercises.prompt: True or false: momentum can help an optimizer glide through a flat plateau where plain SGD's steps would shrink to a crawl.
exercises.explain: True! Built-up velocity keeps carrying the optimizer forward even where the current slope is nearly flat.

exercises.sentence: Momentum blends the current slope direction with the ___ built up from recent steps.
exercises.explain: Velocity is momentum's running memory of the directions recent steps have been heading.

exercises.prompt: Two training runs hit the same steep ravine. Run A's loss bounces wildly step after step. Run B's loss decreases smoothly and quickly through the same ravine. Which run is more likely using momentum?

options.text: Run B — momentum's velocity cancels out the side-to-side bounce and keeps pushing forward along the ravine
Run A — bouncing wildly is a sign of momentum working correctly
Neither — momentum has no effect on ravines

exercises.explain: Right — Run B's smooth, fast descent through the ravine is the signature of momentum canceling the zig-zag.

exercises.prompt: A teammate cranks up momentum's velocity setting as high as possible, hoping training will now blast straight to the best minimum every time. What actually happens, and why?

options.text: Too much velocity can carry the optimizer flying past a good minimum, overshooting it — the ball rolls too fast to settle, so the amount of past velocity to keep has to be tuned, not maxed out
Nothing changes at all — velocity has no effect on the final result
The network becomes instantly perfect with zero error
The loss landscape itself changes shape based on the velocity setting

exercises.explain: Exactly! Momentum smooths the ride, but too much speed causes overshoot. Next up: a smarter version that checks the road ahead before committing to that speed.
